content.full_text:
MARK S. ROSEN
ATTORNEY AT LAW

2107 NORTH BROADWAY, SUITE 202
SANTA ANA, CALIFORNIA 92706

TELEPHONE (714) 972-8040


July 30, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Ste 800
Sacramento, CA 94804-0807

Re: Santa Ana Chamber of Commerce Political
    Action Committee

Dear Sir or Madam:

     I am writing on behalf of the Santa Ana Chamber of
Commerce Political Action Committee, I.D. No. 861113. We
are requesting your opinion pursuant to Government Code
§83114(a) and written advice pursuant to Government Code
§83114(b) so that my client can guide its behavior in the
upcoming election.

     The Santa Ana Chamber of Commerce Political Action
Committee (SACPAC) is the political action committee of the
Santa Ana Chamber of Commerce. This committee has been in
existence for several years. While it has endorsed
candidates for non-partisan offices, it has spent its funds
on its own independent campaign efforts.

     The City of Santa Ana has a general election this
November in which three city council seats are up for
election and the position for mayor is up for election.
The city council has six council seats in all. Council
seats are allocated in districts, although the voters vote
for candidates in each district. The mayor is elected
citywide and serves for two years. SACPAC wishes to do a
mailer in which it endorses three council candidates, one
in each district in which there is an election, and a
candidate for mayor.   The mailer may also include an
application for absentee ballots, which are to be sent to a
SACPAC address. It is the intention of SACPAC to send its
mailer throughout the city.

Fair Political Practices Commission
July 30, 1992
Page Two

SACPAC will independently determine which candidates
it will endorse. However, the person who will design the
mailer may also be the consultant for one or more of
endorsed candidates.

Based on these facts, I believe the following is the
law:

1. SACPAC does not become a state mailer
organization by virtue of using a mailer to endorse four
candidates. SACPAC does not fall within §82048.4(a)(2)
because it did not receive payments for the production of a
state mailer, but instead has received contributions from
its members for use in general campaign purposes. The
concept of endorsing four candidates is a decision made by
SACPAC as a form of campaigning, rather than the sole
reason for the existence of the organization.

2. Because SACPAC is a general purpose committee
(Government Code §82027.5), inclusion of candidates in its
mailer does not constitute a contribution to those
candidates.

3. The possible use of a consultant who also is a
consultant for particular candidates has no effect on the
nature of SACPAC, or on whether the mailer is a
contribution, as long as SACPAC independently decided who
to endorse, and the mailer is prepared with SACPAC's funds.

We set forth these conclusions in the hope that you
will agree with them and will notify us. Please provide us
with an opinion or an advisory letter which we can rely on
in meeting our disclosure and reporting requirements.

A prompt response will be greatly appreciated.

Very truly yours,

MARK S. ROSEN

MSR/pd
